what: The two targets, side by side
title: A1 · Getting started
text: fit object carries population-level site-level quantities. Pulling one table makes contrast explicit. Two inferential targets one fit: prior g (top row) per-site posterior latent effects (rows 1-5). Two patterns read table. First, every posterior estimate falls corresponding raw θ̂\hat\theta_i prior mean gg — shrinkage signature. Site 5, extreme observed estimate (θ̂5=0.61\hat\theta_5 = 0.61), shrinks furthest absolute terms toward prior mean. Second, posterior scale value smaller corresponding σi\sigma_i, reflecting precision gain partial pooling gg.
code: ps         <- fit_summary$prior_summary post_means <- coef(fit)  prior_row <- data.frame(   level    = "Prior g (population)",   site     = NA_integer_,   estimate = ps$mean,   scale    = ps$sd,   note     = "mean and sd of g(theta)" )  site_rows <- data.frame(   level    = "Posterior theta_i (per site)",   site     = seq_along(post_means),   estimate = unname(post_means),   scale    = fit_summary$theta_summary$sd,   note     = "posterior mean and sd" )  knitr::kable(   rbind(prior_row, site_rows),   digits  = 3,   caption = paste(     "Two inferential targets from one fit:",     "the prior g (top row) and the per-site posterior latent",     "effects (rows 1-5)."   ) )

Many applied meta-analyses begin . raw record per-arm summaries — group means, standard deviations, sample sizes — analyst’s first task convert summaries per-site estimate sampling standard error. vignette walks full path: raw aggregate data, metafor::escalc(), as_bef_data() bridge, bayes_efron_fit(), standard outputs.

title: A3 · From metafor::escalc() to bayesEfron
text: Consider randomized education trial run across K=10K = 10 schools. school contributes treatment arm control arm, arm reporting outcome mean, within-arm standard deviation, per-arm student count. outcome standardized end--year test score; program assignment randomized within school. substantive research question two parts. First, population distribution school-level program effects look like — concentrated around common effect, genuinely heterogeneous? Second, individual school’s program effect partial pooling absorbs noise small-sample estimate? raw record following data frame. Column names follow convention used metafor::escalc() two-sample summaries: m1i, sd1i, n1i treatment arm m2i, sd2i, n2i control arm. Treatment-arm means range 0.050.05 0.540.54, control-arm means −0.08-0.08 0.110.11, per-arm sample sizes 3636 4646 students. Three features record matter bridge. two arms site independent random samples, sampling variance per-site mean difference sum two per-arm sampling variances; metafor::escalc() computes analyst. outcome scale standardized test score, mean-difference effect size interpretable scale Cohen’s-dd-style standardized mean difference without additional pooling step. per-arm sample sizes modest enough within-site sampling noise large relative plausible true -site variation — regime hierarchical estimator earns keep.

title: A3 · From metafor::escalc() to bayesEfron
text: metafor package canonical R interface family per-study effect-size sampling-variance computations upstream meta-analysis. escalc() constructor takes group-arm summaries measure argument selects estimator. continuous outcomes common scale measure = "MD" (raw mean difference) natural choice; studies report different outcome scales measure = "SMD" (standardized mean difference, Hedges’s gg) normalize common standard-deviation unit. trial uses single standardized test, raw mean difference want. returned object class c("escalc", "data.frame") carries two new columns alongside original ones. Per-site effect sizes metafor::escalc(measure = "MD"). Column yi treatment-minus-control mean difference; column vi sampling variance, computed sum two per-arm variances mean. Read two new columns directly. yi per-site estimate θ̂=y‾,T−y‾,C\widehat\theta_i = \bar y_{,T} - \bar y_{,C}: treatment-minus-control mean difference site ii. vi corresponding sampling variance Var̂(θ̂)=si,T2/ni,T+si,C2/ni,C\widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(\widehat\theta_i) = s_{,T}^2/n_{,T} + s_{,C}^2/n_{,C}: textbook estimate sampling variance independent-samples mean difference. sampling standard error — quantity bayesEfron needs — σi=vi\sigma_i = \sqrt{v_i}. slab argument records site labels attribute yi; travel escalc’s print method downstream metafor models, escalc promote data frame’s row names. per-site estimates trial run 0.130.13 0.440.44 standard errors roughly 0.210.21 0.270.27, standardized-score scale. classical fixed-effect summary inverse-variance-weighted mean; random-effects summary method--moments REML estimate -site variance. throw away per-site point estimates single pass. bayesEfron’s contribution keep : estimates continuous mixing distribution g(θ)g(\theta) latent site effects returns posterior θi\theta_i separately, addition summaries gg .
code: esc <- metafor::escalc(   measure = "MD",   m1i  = m1i,  sd1i = sd1i, n1i = n1i,   m2i  = m2i,  sd2i = sd2i, n2i = n2i,   data = trial,   slab = site ) class(esc) #> [1] "escalc"     "data.frame" knitr::kable(   as.data.frame(esc)[, c("site", "yi", "vi")],   digits  = 4,   caption = paste(     "Per-site effect sizes from `metafor::escalc(measure = \"MD\")`.",     "Column `yi` is the treatment-minus-control mean difference;",     "column `vi` is its sampling variance, computed as the sum of",     "the two per-arm variances of the mean."   ) )

title: A4 · Choosing a grid

code: grid_long <- function(g, recipe) {   B_norm <- apply(g$B, 2, function(col) {     rng <- range(col)     if (diff(rng) <= 0) col else (col - rng[1]) / diff(rng)   })   data.frame(     recipe = recipe,     x      = rep(g$grid, ncol(B_norm)),     y      = as.numeric(B_norm),     basis  = factor(rep(seq_len(ncol(B_norm)), each = length(g$grid)))   ) }  basis_all <- rbind(   grid_long(g_real, "paper_realdata"),   grid_long(g_sim,  "paper_simulation"),   grid_long(g_sens, "paper_sensitivity"),   grid_long(g_kl,   "kl_target_experimental") ) basis_all$recipe <- factor(   basis_all$recipe,   levels = c(     "paper_realdata", "paper_simulation",     "paper_sensitivity", "kl_target_experimental"   ) )  rug_all <- rbind(   data.frame(recipe = "paper_realdata",         x = g_real$grid),   data.frame(recipe = "paper_simulation",       x = g_sim$grid),   data.frame(recipe = "paper_sensitivity",      x = g_sens$grid),   data.frame(recipe = "kl_target_experimental", x = g_kl$grid) ) rug_all$recipe <- factor(rug_all$recipe, levels = levels(basis_all$recipe))  obs_all <- do.call(rbind, lapply(   levels(basis_all$recipe),   function(r) data.frame(recipe = r, x = theta_hat, y = 0) )) obs_all$recipe <- factor(obs_all$recipe, levels = levels(basis_all$recipe))  ggplot2::ggplot() +   ggplot2::geom_line(     data = basis_all,     ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = y, colour = basis),     alpha = 0.7   ) +   ggplot2::geom_rug(     data = rug_all,     ggplot2::aes(x = x), sides = "b", alpha = 0.25   ) +   ggplot2::geom_point(     data = obs_all,     ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = y), colour = "black", size = 1.5   ) +   ggplot2::facet_wrap(~ recipe, ncol = 2) +   ggplot2::labs(     x = expression(theta),     y = "spline basis (normalized within panel)"   ) +   ggplot2::guides(colour = "none") +   ggplot2::theme_minimal()

code: set.seed(2026) theta_hat_demo <- c(seq(-1.0, 1.0, length.out = 9), -0.8, 0.6, 0.85) sigma_demo     <- rep(0.15, length(theta_hat_demo))  g_obj <- make_efron_grid(   theta_hat = theta_hat_demo,   sigma     = sigma_demo,   L         = 101L,   M         = 6L )  theta_grid <- g_obj$grid g_cont <- function(t) {   0.55 * dnorm(t, mean = -0.4, sd = 0.20) +     0.45 * dnorm(t, mean =  0.6, sd = 0.18) } g_grid <- g_cont(theta_grid) g_grid <- g_grid / sum(g_grid) theta_dense <- seq(min(theta_grid), max(theta_grid), length.out = 401)  if (has_ggplot2) {   df_cont <- data.frame(theta = theta_dense, density = g_cont(theta_dense))   df_grid <- data.frame(theta = theta_grid, mass = g_grid)   ggplot2::ggplot() +     ggplot2::geom_segment(       data = df_grid,       ggplot2::aes(x = theta, xend = theta, y = 0, yend = mass),       colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.4     ) +     ggplot2::geom_line(       data = df_cont,       ggplot2::aes(x = theta, y = density / sum(density) *                      length(theta_dense) / length(theta_grid)),       colour = "black", linewidth = 0.6     ) +     ggplot2::labs(       x = expression(theta),       y = expression(g(theta)),       title = NULL     ) +     ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11) } else {   par(mar = c(4, 4, 1, 1))   plot(theta_grid, g_grid, type = "h", col = "grey40", lwd = 1,        xlab = expression(theta), ylab = expression(g(theta)))   lines(theta_dense,         g_cont(theta_dense) / sum(g_cont(theta_dense)) *           length(theta_dense) / length(theta_grid),         col = "black", lwd = 1.5) }

rng[1]) / diff(rng)   })   data.frame(     recipe = recipe,     x      = rep(g$grid, ncol(B_norm)),     y      = as.numeric(B_norm),     basis  = factor(rep(seq_len(ncol(B_norm)), each = length(g$grid)))   ) }  basis_all <- rbind(   grid_long(g_real, "paper_realdata"),   grid_long(g_sim,  "paper_simulation"),   grid_long(g_sens, "paper_sensitivity"),   grid_long(g_kl,   "kl_target_experimental") ) basis_all$recipe <- factor(   basis_all$recipe,   levels = c(     "paper_realdata", "paper_simulation",     "paper_sensitivity", "kl_target_experimental"   ) )  rug_all <- rbind(   data.frame(recipe = "paper_realdata",         x = g_real$grid),   data.frame(recipe = "paper_simulation",       x = g_sim$grid),   data.frame(recipe = "paper_sensitivity",      x = g_sens$grid),   data.frame(recipe = "kl_target_experimental", x = g_kl$grid) ) rug_all$recipe <- factor(rug_all$recipe, levels = levels(basis_all$recipe))  obs_all <- do.call(rbind, lapply(   levels(basis_all$recipe),   function(r) data.frame(recipe = r, x = theta_hat, y = 0) )) obs_all$recipe <- factor(obs_all$recipe, levels = levels(basis_all$recipe))  ggplot2::ggplot() +   ggplot2::geom_line(     data = basis_all,     ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = y, colour = basis),     alpha = 0.7   ) +   ggplot2::geom_rug(     data = rug_all,     ggplot2::aes(x = x), sides = "b", alpha = 0.25   ) +   ggplot2::geom_point(     data = obs_all,     ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = y), colour = "black", size = 1.5   ) +   ggplot2::facet_wrap(~ recipe, ncol = 2) +   ggplot2::labs(     x = expression(theta),     y = "spline basis (normalized within panel)"   ) +   ggplot2::guides(colour = "none") +   ggplot2::theme_minimal()

Stan function log_softmax(x) returns x−log⁡∑jexp⁡(xj)x - \log\!\sum_j \exp(x_j), computed via log-sum-exp identity subtracts max⁡jxj\max_j x_j exponentiation, normalisation numerically stable even BαB\alpha contains large positive large negative entries. model block consumes log_g (log space, per-site log-marginal likelihood), generated-quantities block consumes log_g (per-site posterior grid point) g (moments E[θ]\mathrm{E}[\theta], Var[θ]\mathrm{Var}[\theta], SD[θ]\mathrm{SD}[\theta]). title: M5 · Stan model, sampler, and cache
text: model block specifies joint p(𝛉̂,α,λ)p(\hat{\boldsymbol\theta}, \alpha, \lambda) normalising constant. contains two prior statements one likelihood loop. hyperprior smoothness precision half-Cauchy location zero scale five, λ∼Half-Cauchy(0,5)\lambda \sim \mathrm{Half\text{-}Cauchy}(0, 5), weakly informative sense Gelman (2006, Bayesian Analysis): heavy tails permit data dominate spline coefficients support large magnitudes, truncation λ>0\lambda > 0 excludes ill-posed limit. Conditional λ\lambda, prior spline coefficients independent Gaussian mean zero standard deviation λ−1/2\lambda^{-1/2}, written Stan source alpha ~ normal(0, inv_sqrt(lambda)). implied marginal prior α\alpha integrating λ\lambda heavier tails fixed-precision Gaussian; mechanism model adapts effective smoothness gg data without analyst-chosen ridge constant. likelihood loop =1,…,Ki = 1, \ldots, K implements discrete mixture marginal M3: p(θ̂∣σi,g)=∑ℓ=1Lgℓφ(θ̂;θℓ,σi), p(\hat\theta_i \mid \sigma_i, g)   \;=\; \sum_{\ell=1}^{L} g_\ell \, \varphi(\hat\theta_i;\, \theta_\ell,\, \sigma_i),  φ(⋅;μ,σ)\varphi(\cdot;\, \mu, \sigma) Gaussian density. loop builds length-LL vector log-components log⁡gℓ+log⁡φ(θ̂;θℓ,σi)\log g_\ell + \log \varphi(\hat\theta_i;\, \theta_\ell,\, \sigma_i) reduces log_sum_exp, evaluates log⁡∑ℓexp⁡(xℓ)\log\!\sum_\ell \exp(x_\ell) m+log⁡∑ℓexp⁡(xℓ−m)m + \log\!\sum_\ell \exp(x_\ell - m) m=max⁡ℓxℓm = \max_\ell x_\ell. shift guarantees largest exponentiated term exactly one, underflow impossible dominant component catastrophic cancellation naive ∑exp\sum \exp suffer small σi\sigma_i avoided. contribution target log marginal log⁡p(θ̂∣σi,g)\log p(\hat\theta_i \mid \sigma_i, g); cumulative target loop log joint normalising constant gg already absorbed log_softmax. manual Jacobian adjustment required: constrained parameter λ>0\lambda > 0, Stan applies half-real Jacobian automatically.
code: model {   // ============================================================================   // PRIORS   // ============================================================================      // Hyperprior on regularization parameter   // Half-Cauchy(0, 5) is weakly informative, allowing adaptation to data   lambda ~ cauchy(0, 5);      // Conditional prior on spline coefficients   // Ridge penalty with variance 1/lambda encourages smoothness   alpha ~ normal(0, inv_sqrt(lambda));      // ============================================================================   // LIKELIHOOD   // ============================================================================      // Mixture likelihood for observed effects   // Each θ_hat_i comes from mixture: Σ_j g_j × Normal(grid_j, sigma_i)   for (i in 1:K) {     vector[L] log_components;          // Compute log-likelihood for each mixture component     for (j in 1:L) {       log_components[j] = log_g[j]  // Prior weight for component j                         + normal_lpdf(theta_hat[i] | grid[j], sigma[i]);     }          // Add log marginal likelihood using log-sum-exp for numerical stability     target += log_sum_exp(log_components);   } }

title: M5 · Stan model, sampler, and cache
text: generated-quantities block computes nine summaries per posterior draw. release-blocking Tier 0 verification target tier0_gq_count asserts exactly count. nine generated quantities partition three groups. first group records moments estimated mixing distribution gg: mean_g =∑ℓgℓθℓ= \sum_\ell g_\ell \theta_\ell, posterior mean gg viewed distribution; var_g =∑ℓgℓ(θℓ−mean_g)2= \sum_\ell g_\ell (\theta_\ell - \mathrm{mean\_g})^2, two-pass variance; sd_g =var_g= \sqrt{\mathrm{var\_g}}. Per-draw summaries three quantities populate metadata fields mean_g_summary, var_g_summary, sd_g_summary attribute carried fit$metadata. second group computes per-site posterior summaries. site ii log posterior grid log⁡p(θℓ∣θ̂,g)\log p(\theta_\ell \mid \hat\theta_i, g) formed log_g plus per-grid-point Gaussian log-likelihood, normalised via max-shift log_sum_exp discipline. theta_map selects grid index maximises log posterior; theta_mean posterior mean ∑ℓwℓθℓ\sum_\ell w_\ell \theta_\ell wℓw_\ell normalised posterior weights; theta_sd two-pass posterior standard deviation; theta_rep draw discrete posterior via categorical_rng(w), per-draw replicate used posterior predictive intervals (theta_summary theta_rep_draws metadata fields). third group computes model diagnostics: effective_params =K−∑iVar(θi∣θ̂,g)/σi2= K - \sum_i \mathrm{Var}(\theta_i \mid \hat\theta_i, g)/\sigma_i^2, effective-parameter count analogous hierarchical DIC\mathrm{DIC} penalty, log_marginal_likelihood =∑ilog⁡p(θ̂∣σi,g)= \sum_i \log p(\hat\theta_i \mid \sigma_i, g), log data marginal discrete mixture. feed effective_params_summary log_marginal_likelihood_summary metadata fields.
code: generated quantities {   // ============================================================================   // PRIOR DISTRIBUTION SUMMARIES   // ============================================================================      // Moments of estimated prior g(θ)   real mean_g = dot_product(g, grid);                     // E[θ]   real var_g = dot_product(g, square(grid - mean_g));     // Var[θ]   real sd_g = sqrt(var_g);                                // SD[θ]      // ============================================================================   // POSTERIOR SUMMARIES FOR SITE-SPECIFIC EFFECTS   // ============================================================================      // Three types of posterior summaries for each site   vector[K] theta_map;   // Maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimates   vector[K] theta_mean;  // Posterior means (optimal under squared error loss)   vector[K] theta_rep;   // Posterior draws (for credible intervals)      // Additional posterior uncertainty measures   vector[K] theta_sd;    // Posterior standard deviations      for (i in 1:K) {     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------     // Compute posterior distribution for site i     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------          vector[L] log_post;  // Log posterior at each grid point          // Bayes' rule: posterior ∝ prior × likelihood     for (j in 1:L) {       log_post[j] = log_g[j]  // Log prior                   + normal_lpdf(theta_hat[i] | grid[j], sigma[i]);  // Log likelihood     }          // ------------------------------------------------------------------------     // MAP estimate (posterior mode)     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------          int max_idx = 1;     for (j in 2:L) {       if (log_post[j] > log_post[max_idx]) {         max_idx = j;       }     }     theta_map[i] = grid[max_idx];          // ------------------------------------------------------------------------     // Normalize posterior to get weights     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------          // Use log-sum-exp trick for numerical stability     real log_post_max = max(log_post);     vector[L] w = exp(log_post - log_post_max);     w = w / sum(w);  // Now w contains posterior probabilities          // ------------------------------------------------------------------------     // Posterior mean     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------          theta_mean[i] = dot_product(w, grid);          // ------------------------------------------------------------------------     // Posterior standard deviation     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------          real second_moment = dot_product(w, square(grid));     theta_sd[i] = sqrt(second_moment - square(theta_mean[i]));          // ------------------------------------------------------------------------     // Posterior draw (for uncertainty quantification)     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------          // Sample from discrete posterior distribution     theta_rep[i] = grid[categorical_rng(w)];   }      // ============================================================================   // MODEL DIAGNOSTICS AND CHECKS   // ============================================================================      // Effective number of parameters (for model comparison)   real effective_params;   {     vector[K] posterior_vars;     for (i in 1:K) {       // Compute posterior variance for each site       vector[L] log_post;       for (j in 1:L) {         log_post[j] = log_g[j] + normal_lpdf(theta_hat[i] | grid[j], sigma[i]);       }       real log_post_max = max(log_post);       vector[L] w = exp(log_post - log_post_max);       w = w / sum(w);              real post_mean = dot_product(w, grid);       real post_second_moment = dot_product(w, square(grid));       posterior_vars[i] = post_second_moment - square(post_mean);     }          // Effective parameters = K - sum of shrinkage factors     effective_params = K - sum(posterior_vars ./ square(sigma));   }      // Log marginal likelihood (for model comparison)   real log_marginal_likelihood = 0;   for (i in 1:K) {     vector[L] log_components;     for (j in 1:L) {       log_components[j] = log_g[j] + normal_lpdf(theta_hat[i] | grid[j], sigma[i]);     }     log_marginal_likelihood += log_sum_exp(log_components);   } }

title: bayesEfron — Fully Bayesian Efron log-spline deconvolution
text: Fully Bayesian Efron log-spline deconvolution univariate random-effects meta-analysis heteroscedastic standard errors. arrive per-site effect estimates θ̂\hat\theta_i within-study standard errors σi\sigma_i, one pair per study, σi\sigma_i free vary across sites. estimates noisy realizations latent site effects θi\theta_i drawn unknown mixing distribution g(θ)g(\theta). conventional random-effects meta-analysis collapses gg Gaussian summarized single heterogeneity parameter, discards information whenever latent effects skewed, heavy-tailed, multimodal. bayesEfron returns two posterior objects single fit. first continuous nonparametric estimate g(θ)g(\theta) , expressed Efron’s log-spline prior fixed grid effect-size values. second per-site posterior θi∣θ̂,σi\theta_i \mid \hat\theta_i, \sigma_i — empirical-Bayes shrunken estimates borrow strength estimated gg. Deconvolution operation recovers gg noisy (θ̂,σi)(\hat\theta_i, \sigma_i) pairs; makes per-site posteriors honest population drawn .

title: bayesEfron — Fully Bayesian Efron log-spline deconvolution
text: Fully Bayesian inference. spline coefficients g(θ)g(\theta) sampled jointly latent θi\theta_i, propagating uncertainty gg every per-site posterior — plug-empirical-Bayes point estimate gg followed separate conditional step. Exact heteroscedasticity. site enters likelihood σi\sigma_i; model never collapses within-study standard errors single pooled value meta-analytic typical variance. Two-tier compile cache. first fit compiles Stan model stores binary -session cache -disk cache keyed model source, package version, CmdStan version, platform. Later fits matching keys skip compilation entirely.

title: bayesEfron: Fully Bayesian Inference for the Empirical-Bayes Deconvolution Problem — bayesEfron-package
text: bayesEfron fits fully Bayesian Efron log-spline prior univariate random-effects meta-analytic deconvolution heteroscedastic within-study standard errors. package applied meta-analyst vector study-level effect estimates standard errors wants two related quantities: posterior summaries latent site effects, continuous, smoothly regularised estimate underlying mixing distribution generated effects. objects returned single fit, calibrated credible intervals derived posterior draws.

title: bayesEfron: Fully Bayesian Inference for the Empirical-Bayes Deconvolution Problem — bayesEfron-package
text: sites \(= 1, \ldots, K\), $$\hat\theta_i \mid \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i, \sigma_i^2),   \qquad \theta_i \mid g \sim g,$$ \(g\) unknown mixing distribution. package represents \(g\) discrete distribution fixed grid length \(L\) (default \(L = 101\)) whose log-density linear combination \(M\) natural-cubic-spline basis functions (default \(M = 6\)). spline coefficients receive weakly informative Gaussian prior whose precision \(\lambda\) turn assigned half-Cauchy hyperprior, smoothness deconvolved density part sampler learns. Posterior computation delegated CmdStan via cmdstanr package, two-tier compile caching repeated fits model code reuse compiled binary. within-study standard errors enter exactly: site's likelihood contribution uses \(\sigma_i\), homoscedasticity assumption.

title: bayesEfron: Fully Bayesian Inference for the Empirical-Bayes Deconvolution Problem — bayesEfron-package
text: bayes_efron_fit() Primary fitting entry. Takes per-site effect estimates standard errors, runs eight-stage pipeline (validation, grid construction, Stan-data preparation, cache-backed model retrieval, sampling, draw extraction, postprocessing, assembly), returns fitted bef_fit_re object carrying posterior draws, deconvolved density, posterior summaries site effects. make_efron_grid() Constructs discrete support \(g\) natural-cubic-spline basis evaluated . Four recipes: paper real-data, paper simulation, paper sensitivity, experimental KL-target recipe. as_bef_data() Input adapter. Converts plain list theta_hat/sigma, metafor::escalc() object, existing bef_data object canonical input class. diagnose() Diagnostic producer. Returns bef_diagnostic object summarising R-hat, effective sample sizes, divergent transitions, max-treedepth saturations, sampler-health quantities.

title: Fit the Bayesian Efron random-effects model — bayes_efron_fit
text: Fit fully Bayesian Efron log-spline prior univariate random-effects meta-analytic deconvolution problem. Given per-site effect estimates within-study standard errors, bayes_efron_fit() returns posterior site-effect summaries together continuous estimate underlying mixing distribution. function targets applied meta-analysts already point estimates standard errors comparable scale (example, yi sqrt(vi) columns metafor::escalc() object). title: Construct an Efron log-spline grid — make_efron_grid
code: theta_hat <- c(-0.45, -0.10, 0.20, 0.55, 0.90) sigma     <- c( 0.20,  0.18, 0.22, 0.16, 0.24)  # Default real-data grid: endpoints from observed range + 50 percent expansion. g_real <- make_efron_grid(theta_hat, sigma) length(g_real$grid)            # 101 by default #> [1] 101 dim(g_real$B)                  # L x M #> [1] 101   6 g_real$grid_method #> [1] "paper_realdata"  # Simulation grid (oracle required): pretend the truth is known. theta_true <- c(-0.40, 0.00, 0.10, 0.70, 1.00) g_sim <- make_efron_grid(   theta_hat   = theta_hat,   sigma       = sigma,   theta_true  = theta_true,   grid_method = "paper_simulation" )  # Sensitivity grid: widen oracle bounds by `bound_expansion`. g_sens <- make_efron_grid(   theta_hat       = theta_hat,   sigma           = sigma,   theta_true      = theta_true,   grid_method     = "paper_sensitivity",   bound_expansion = 0.5 )  # Experimental KL-target recipe: emits a once-per-session disclaimer. g_kl <- make_efron_grid(   theta_hat   = theta_hat,   sigma       = sigma,   grid_method = "kl_target_experimental" ) #> kl_target_experimental: ebnm KL bound assumes homoscedastic observations (`grid_selection.R:60-63`); calibration on heteroscedastic input is not validated against any published number. #> kl_target_experimental grid length set to 51 to enforce [51, 300] bounds.

title: bayesEfron 0.1.0
text: bayes_efron_fit() main fitting entry point: takes per-site effect estimates within-study standard errors, samples log-spline mixing distribution g(θ)g(\theta) jointly per-site latent effects θi\theta_i, returns bef_fit_re object carrying posterior draws, summaries, generated quantities, sampler diagnostics, reproducibility metadata. make_efron_grid() constructs discrete support natural cubic-spline basis g(θ)g(\theta) ships four named recipes — paper_realdata, paper_simulation, paper_sensitivity, experimental kl_target_experimental — recording formula source attribution returned object. as_bef_data() input converter; accepts existing bef_data objects, plain lists (theta_hat, sigma), metafor::escalc() output, metafor aggregate-data workflow flows directly bayesEfron fit. bayes_efron_compile() pre-warms CmdStan model cache runs short post-compile smoke check, bayes_efron_clear_cache() provides idempotent cleanup four scopes (lock_only, session, compiled_models, ) managing -disk cache sessions across CmdStan upgrades. Fitted random-effects objects support standard model-object surface: summary(), coef(), vcov(), confint(), .data.frame(), nobs(), logLik(), posterior::as_draws(), plus diagnose() tidy bef_diagnostic view sampler convergence posterior predictive flags. plot() fitted random-effects objects offers caterpillar, prior-summary, single-fit sensitivity, diagnostic views, base-graphics default optional ggplot2 backend (BAYESEFRON_NO_GGPLOT2=1 forces base). format() print() methods bef_fit, summary.bef_fit, bef_data, bef_diagnostic use cli formatting available fall back plain base output BAYESEFRON_NO_CLI=1, code prints cleanly interactive log-capture contexts.

title: bayesEfron 0.1.0
text: v0.1 scope intentionally restricted random-effects model: non-RE model families, grouped designs, within-cluster correlations rejected input boundary rather silently reinterpreted. v0.1.0 release gate Tier 3 calibration theta_rep interval coverage Lee–Sui benchmark, twenty replications run per K aggregate coverage required fall inside pre-specified acceptance interval [0.87, 0.92] K ∈\{50, 100, 200, 500, 1500}. K = 1500 result thin worst case, aggregate coverage 0.876433333333333 — inside [0.87, 0.92] recorded documented release evidence. Tier 3 live verification environment-variable gated. BAYESEFRON_RUN_LIVE=1 opts smoke path, BAYESEFRON_RUN_FULL_LIVE=1 opts full five-K twenty-replication release path, fresh full-live refit without accepted replay matrix additionally requires BAYESEFRON_TIER3_OK_TO_REFIT=1. Default test runs tag-CI replays launch new full Stan recalibration. Deferred v0.2 later: correlated hierarchical effects (CHE), meta-regression, multivariate outcomes, alternate priors gg, empirical-Bayes fallback path. None exposed v0.1.
